I am no one special but take it from someone who actually kniws this stuff.  In this show, the delineation of authority between various arms of the National Park Service (NPS), particularly the Investigative Services Branch (ISB) and the Yosemite rangers, is frequently misrepresented. The series portrays rangers and ISB agents as interchangeable enforcers, each exercising sweeping investigative powers without procedural oversight. In reality, jurisdiction within a national park is tightly defined. Rangers possess law enforcement authority, but their investigative powers are limited and often require coordination with federal agencies such as the FBI or the Department of the Interior. The show’s suggestion that a park ranger might independently conduct homicide inquiries, confront armed suspects, and bypass established chains of command undermines the complexity of federal jurisdiction in protected lands. This dramatization not only diminishes the procedural rigor of NPS operations but also risks conveying to the public a false impression of unilateral ranger authority.

From an investigative standpoint, Untamed sacrifices accuracy for narrative expedience. The series frequently compresses timelines to an implausible degree, presenting forensic analyses, victim identification, and ballistics reports as though they can be resolved within hours. In reality, such processes require days or even weeks, particularly when conducted in the remote conditions characteristic of Yosemite. Furthermore, the portrayal of evidence handling is troublingly casual. Investigators in the show handle potentially contaminated objects without gloves, neglect chain-of-custody documentation, and operate in isolation rather than as coordinated teams. These inaccuracies create a distorted view of federal investigations, which in practice are meticulous, bureaucratically layered, and contingent upon inter-agency cooperation. By neglecting the procedural integrity of real investigations, the series undermines the credibility of its mystery and trivializes the discipline of federal law enforcement.

Perhaps the most glaring inaccuracies in Untamed arise in its depiction of climbing culture and risk on El Capitan. The series frequently shows climbers engaging in reckless behavior that no competent mountaineer would condone. For example, harnesses are donned casually after being left unattended on the ground, ropes are clipped in ways that disregard redundancy, and belay practices are either omitted or portrayed incorrectly. The narrative also exaggerates the dramatic potential of falls: while a slip on El Capitan is indeed fatal without protection, the injuries depicted often ignore the physics of rope systems and the realities of fall factors. Moreover, climbers in Yosemite are renowned for their meticulous safety checks and community awareness, yet the show presents them as solitary, careless individuals driven by drama rather than discipline. These misrepresentations may serve the plot, but they betray the authenticity of climbing culture and risk misinforming audiences about the seriousness and technical precision inherent in big-wall climbing.

Within the first ten minutes of Untamed, the series fatally undermines its own authenticity through a glaring misrepresentation of working at height safety. The depiction of a climber casually walking up to a cliff and strapping into a harness that has been lying unattended on the ground is not simply careless storytelling, it is an insult to the disciplines of climbing andnworking at heights. No climber in their right mind would ever entrust their life to gear without at least a cursory inspection, checking for frayed stitching, compromised buckles, or contamination from dirt and grit. This lapse is not a minor quibble but a fundamental betrayal of the climbing community’s culture of precision and safety, and it instantly shatters the suspension of disbelief for anyone even remotely familiar with working at heights and/or big-wall climbing.

This series is loosely based on something that happened to me a few years back.  I was climbing in Yosemite when a stray bullet from a hunter went through my leg and caused me to fall.  The fall broke my back, and I lingered for more than two days before park Rangers rescued me.  Everything else in the show is just made up, but it is fascinating for me to see  how my real life experience led to a Netflix series.  And, hey, don’t even get me started on how my experience in high school mirrors Stranger Things.  Life is weird sometimes.

Critique of Untamed (2025): A Disjointed Mystery That Fails to Track

Netflix’s Untamed (2025), a six-episode miniseries set in Yosemite National Park, promises a gripping murder mystery anchored by Eric Bana’s compelling performance as Kyle Turner, a National Park Service Investigative Services Branch (ISB) agent. While the series boasts a stunning backdrop and a strong cast, its narrative stumbles, particularly in its handling of the central murder mystery involving a Jane Doe. The opening sequence and subsequent investigation raise more questions than answers, leaving viewers frustrated by plot holes and inconsistencies that undermine the story’s coherence.

The series begins with a visceral, adrenaline-pumping scene: two climbers scaling El Capitan are nearly killed when a young woman’s body plummets from above, becoming entangled in their ropes. The body, later identified as Jane Doe (revealed as a runaway named Grace), is described as already dead before her fall, with a bullet wound in her leg suggesting foul play. The way her body is "tied properly" to the rope is a puzzling detail that sparks the investigation. This setup is intriguing, as it implies a deliberate act—someone must have secured her body to the rope and thrown it off the cliff. Yet, the series fails to adequately explore this critical element, leaving viewers questioning the logistics and plausibility of the crime scene.

The investigation into how Jane Doe was tied to the rope is one of the series’ most perplexing aspects. The show suggests that the precise knotting and positioning of the body indicate a calculated act, yet it never fully explains why this detail is so significant or how it was executed. If the killer tied her body and threw it off El Capitan, wouldn’t they have been nearby during or shortly before the climbers’ ascent? 

Eric Bana’s Kyle Turner, portrayed as an expert tracker with a keen eye for wilderness clues, should theoretically be able to find traces of the perpetrator—footprints, disturbed vegetation, or other evidence in the rugged terrain. Bana’s performance is a highlight, with his stoic yet haunted demeanor grounding the series. He embodies Turner’s trauma and determination effectively, making it all the more frustrating that the script doesn’t give him more to work with. Turner’s tracking skills, emphasized as a key trait, are underutilized in resolving the mystery of the killer’s presence or escape, which feels like a missed opportunity in a story set in the vast, untamed wilderness of Yosemite.

The lack of physical evidence or traces of the killer is particularly jarring. The series establishes Yosemite as a place where “things happen different out here,” with only a small percentage of the park’s million acres accessible to tourists. A skilled killer familiar with the park, as the narrative suggests, should still leave some trail for a tracker like Turner to follow. Yet, the investigation veers into convoluted subplots involving drug trafficking and Turner’s personal trauma—namely, the loss of his son Caleb and his fractured marriage to Jill (Rosemarie DeWitt). These detours dilute the focus on the central crime, and the resolution, revealed in the finale, feels overly tidy and predictable. The killer’s identity and motives, tied to a park insider, lack the complexity promised by the opening, and the explanation of Jane Doe’s death (a mix of accidental shooting and her own desperate act) feels unsatisfying given the initial emphasis on the rope and the fall.

Critics have noted similar issues. Slate praises Bana’s steady star power and the Yosemite setting but acknowledges the plot’s familiarity, while IndieWire criticizes the series for leaning on tired crime tropes instead of capitalizing on its unique premise. The Guardian calls it “perfectly serviceable” but predictable, and Roger Ebert highlights the ensemble’s ability to overcome convoluted plotting, yet admits the finale’s twists feel forced. These reviews align with the sense that Untamed doesn’t add up: the intriguing setup of a tied, dead body plummeting from El Capitan is squandered by a narrative that fails to explore its own mysteries with depth or logic.

Ultimately, Untamed is a visually striking series with a strong lead in Eric Bana, but its narrative inconsistencies and undercooked investigation undermine its potential. The question of how and why Jane Doe’s body was tied and thrown remains inadequately addressed, and the lack of tangible clues for a skilled tracker like Turner feels implausible in the context of Yosemite’s wild terrain. For a series that begins with such a shocking premise, Untamed tames itself into a standard, forgettable crime drama, leaving viewers wanting more from both its mystery and its wilderness.
